message:
perf(memdb): apply dense-rowid offset shortcut to range bounds via rowid_lower_bound

Continuation of b4d91cb5's dense_rowid_offset() optimization. The two
range scanners on MemTable (count_rowid_range and
iter_rows_in_rowid_range) were still computing their start/end
positions with a manual binary_search_by_key, paying the O(log n)
cost on every call. Both functions are on the hot path for any
indexed range scan against the memdb mirror.

rowid_lower_bound(rowid) is the natural bridge between
find_by_rowid()'s "is this row present?" semantics and the range
scanners' "where would this rowid land?" semantics:
- Tries dense_rowid_offset(rowid) first. If the table is dense
  (autoincrement-style rowids, the common case for memdb-mirrored
  fresh tables), this returns the index in O(1). Note: the row at
  that index may or may not equal rowid; for the range-bounds use
  case, dense_rowid_offset returning Some(idx) means the offset is
  in-range and starts the bound at the right place.
- Falls back to binary_search_by_key. Both Ok(idx) and Err(idx)
  collapse to the same lower-bound semantics — Ok means the rowid
  was found at idx, Err means it would be inserted at idx, and the
  caller wants idx in either case.

Replaced the inline `match self.rows.binary_search_by_key(...) { Ok |
Err => idx }` blocks in both range functions with rowid_lower_bound
calls, removing two 8-line match expressions in favor of one-line
helper invocations. The iter version still does
end.max(start) afterwards to guard against the upper bound resolving
behind the lower bound (a caller error, but defensive).

Test test_memtable_rowid_range_bounds_use_dense_offset_with_sparse_fallback
seeds rowids 10..15, asserts count and iter for [11,14) return 3 and
the right values via the dense path, then deletes rowid 12 (creating
a sparse table) and asserts the same range now yields 2 rows ([11,
13]) via the binary-search fallback. Confirms the dense-fast-path
short-circuit is correctly bypassed when offsets stop being trustworthy.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
